note_html:
Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presents with a 2-hour history of low back pain and tingling sensation in his right leg. The pain started after raking leaves in his yard yesterday and has not improved with rest, ice, or over-the-counter pain medication. The patient reports that the pain is worse when standing up and better when sitting or lying down. His partner also reports that the patient has pain when standing up.

Past medical history: The patient has had a similar episode of back pain 3 years ago that resolved on its own.

Review of systems: The patient reports tingling sensation in his right leg.

Current medications: None mentioned.

Objective:

Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient has some pain on flexion and palpation around the L5 region. Dorsiflexion is normal, and pulses are equal in all extremities.

Test Results: X-ray of the low back shows normal results.

Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient has a probable strain of the low back.

Problem list: Low back strain; suspected nerve compression; [unknown].

Plan:

I prescribed meloxicam 7.5 mg orally twice a day for pain and inflammation. I also prescribed physical therapy to help with stretches and exercises to improve the patient's mobility and reduce pain. I instructed the patient to rest and avoid strenuous activities for the next two weeks. I will re-evaluate the patient in two weeks and consider ordering an MRI if the symptoms do not improve.

Follow-up: I instructed the patient to follow up with me in two weeks to re-evaluate his symptoms and consider further imaging if necessary.
